entries.title: The Trump administration announced a new green card policy Friday that, if carried out as intended, would force most applicants for permanent residency to leave the country and apply from abroad
Here are three political story lines to watch this week: Texas face-off, Trump’s health and the Iran talks. Read more in today’s WSJ Politics newsletter:
NYC’s wealthiest congressional district has a “once-in-a-lifetime” open seat
Texas Sen. John Cornyn’s primary runoff against firebrand state Attorney General Ken Paxton this week is being closely watched as a barometer of the Republican Party’s future in Texas and the country
For more than a year, Republican senators had largely been deferential to President Trump‘s wishes. This week, they revolted over a Trump endorsement and his $1.776 billion “anti-weaponization fund.”
President Trump postponed signing an order on the dangers of AI, heeding an adviser’s warning that industry guardrails could slow down U.S. models in the race with tools from China
Most green-card applicants will need to go abroad to apply for permanent residency at an American consulate, rather than filing from within the U.S. as they do now
House Republicans Open Investigation Into Prediction-Market Insider Trading
As the Trump administration faces a barrage of bipartisan criticism for creating a payout system without precedent, potential applicants are eyeing a piece of the pie
President Trump and the GOP-controlled Senate were on a collision course Thursday over the administration’s controversial ‘anti-weaponization fund’
Trump and the GOP fight over his $1.8 billion fund, and “The Sound of Music” helps U.S.-China diplomacy. Read more in today’s What’s News newsletter:
The DNC’s long awaited release of an autopsy report of the 2024 election was met with heavy sighs and deepening frustration across the party
Spencer Pratt, the former reality TV star of “The Hills,” is running a biting, social-media-savvy campaign to be the next mayor of Los Angeles
President Trump delivered a clear message to Republicans in Tuesday’s primaries: Cross the president at your own peril

A Georgia congressman and a former college football coach will meet in a runoff next month after neither captured 50% of the vote in the GOP Senate primary Tuesday to challenge Democrat Jon Ossoff for his Senate seat
President Trump’s handpicked House candidate defeated longtime GOP antagonist Rep. Thomas Massie of Kentucky, underscoring the president’s iron grip on the Republican Party
Key moments from Tulsi Gabbard’s difficult tenure as the top U.S. intelligence officer
China and Russia have in recent years expanded their intelligence operations in Cuba, investing in facilities used to spy on U.S. military sites in Florida, according to officials
Trump said he plans to send 5,000 troops to Poland, a move that is intended to reassure an ally
Republican and Democratic lawmakers on Friday assailed the Pentagon’s decision to cancel the deployment of an armored brigade to Poland, saying the move caught them and U.S. allies by surprise
Pentagon leaders say they could start running out of money for operations this summer unless Congress passes a new wartime spending bill
